title: Covariance: The Leftover of the Product Rule

beats.prompt: Back in Expected Value L2 you met the product rule. Match each statement to its value.

feedback.correct: Product rule holds exactly under independence, so two separate dice have Cov=0.
hints: One card is the EV-L2 product-rule condition; the other is what it does to the leftover.
The product rule is allowed iff the variables are independent; separate dice never influence each other.
Match: 'E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] when…' → 'X, Y are independent'; 'Two independent fair dice: Cov(X₁,X₂)' → '0' (#2).

beats.prompt: Two independent fair six-sided dice, X₁ and X₂. Before computing: what is Cov(X₁,X₂)?

options: Positive — both are dice, so they move together
Can't tell without the joint table

Positive — both are dice, so they move t.note: Identically-distributed isn't dependent. Two separate dice share a distribution but never influence each other; E[X₁X₂]−E[X₁]E[X₂] collapses to 0 (#2).

Can't tell without the joint table.note: Generally true, but independence hands it to you: E[X₁X₂]=E[X₁]E[X₂] exactly, so the leftover is 0 (#2).

Zero — they're independent.note: Independence makes the product rule exact, so the leftover vanishes. Cov=0 (#2).

beats.prompt: Quick primer before we explore — read, then continue.

interaction.title: What is a joint pmf?
interaction.body: A joint probability mass function (joint pmf) assigns a probability P(X=x, Y=y) to every pair of outcomes. The full table has one cell per (x,y) pair; all cells sum to 1. When X and Y are independent, each cell equals P(X=x)·P(Y=y) — the row marginal times the column marginal. When they are dependent, some cells are heavier (or lighter) than that product.

feedback.correct: Joint pmf: one cell per outcome pair; cells sum to 1; independence means row×col.
hints: Read the card.
Each cell holds P(X=x, Y=y); summing all cells gives 1.
Independence means P(X=x, Y=y)=P(X=x)·P(Y=y) for every cell — continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Tap the 2×2 joint-pmf cells to shift probability mass. Watch E[XY], E[X]E[Y], and Cov update live. Try to make Cov negative, then make it exactly 0 without putting all mass in one cell.

hero.structuralReadout: Matched table: Cov(X,Y) = 1/4 — the product rule's leftover is not zero.

feedback.correct: You built independence — the leftover is 0. Move mass onto the diagonal and watch it climb back to 1/4.
hints: Tapping a cell moves mass; the sign chip shows + (diagonal-heavy) or − (anti-diagonal-heavy).
Anti-diagonal mass (X high when Y low) drives Cov negative; equal mass in each cell (the independent layout) zeros it.
Start from the matched preset: Cov=1/4. Move all mass to equal cells for independence → Cov=0.

beats.prompt: Matched bits: P(X=0,Y=0)=P(X=1,Y=1)=1/2, all other cells 0. You are given E[XY]=1/2 and E[X]E[Y]=1/4. What is Cov(X,Y)?

feedback.correct: Cov=E[XY]−E[X]E[Y]=1/2−1/4=1/4 — the leftover isn't zero, this table isn't independent.
hints: Cov is the leftover — find E[XY] and E[X]E[Y] separately, then subtract.
XY=1 only on (1,1) at prob 1/2 → E[XY]=1/2; each bit is Bernoulli(1/2) → E[X]E[Y]=1/4; subtract.

beats.prompt: You just computed it. Now name it.

lenses.label: The formula
lenses.body: Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] − E[X]E[Y]. Subtract the product of the means from the expected product.

lenses.label: The sign
lenses.body: Positive Cov: X and Y tend to be large together. Negative Cov: large X pairs with small Y. Zero: the product rule holds exactly — the variables are independent.

lenses.label: The leftover
lenses.body: E[XY]=E[X]E[Y] is the product rule — it holds only under independence. Covariance is what remains when the rule breaks: it measures how far the joint distribution strays from the independent product.

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: Cov(X,Y)=0 does NOT imply independence (only the converse: independence ⇒ Cov=0). The matched-bits example here achieves ρ=1 (perfect linear dependence), the extreme; the general bound is |ρ|≤1 by Cauchy–Schwarz. For continuous distributions, Cov(X,Y)=∫∫ (x−μX)(y−μY)·f(x,y)dx dy — the same leftover interpretation survives the integral.

feedback.correct: Cov is the leftover of the product rule.
hints: Read each lens card.
The formula subtracts the independent baseline E[X]E[Y] from the observed E[XY].
Tap to continue.

beats.prompt: Retrieval: match each statement to its right-hand side.

feedback.correct: Independent evidence multiplies the likelihoods — the same factorization that forces Cov=0.
hints: Bayes L3: independent likelihoods → ?; and what does independence do to the Cov formula?
Joint factors P(A,B)=P(A)P(B) ⇒ multiply ⇒ Cov=0. The correction term in Cov is E[X]E[Y].
multiply (joint factors) ⇒ Cov=0; correction term = E[X]E[Y].

beats.prompt: Mastery challenge — two joint pmf tables. Compute Cov and, where applicable, ρ.

interaction.scenario: **Part A** Independent fair bits: P(X=0,Y=0)=P(X=0,Y=1)=P(X=1,Y=0)=P(X=1,Y=1)=1/4. **Part B** Matched bits: P(X=0,Y=0)=P(X=1,Y=1)=1/2, other cells 0. For Part B also find ρ=Cov/√(Var(X)·Var(Y)); note Var(X)=Var(Y)=1/4.

feedback.correct: Independent table → Cov=0; matched table breaks the product rule → Cov=1/4, ρ=1. Cov=0 is a result, not a default.
hints: Run Cov=E[XY]−E[X]E[Y] on each table separately — don't assume they match.
A: independent ⇒ leftover 0. B: mass on (0,0),(1,1) ⇒ E[XY]=1/2≠1/4 ⇒ Cov=1/4; ρ=(1/4)/(1/4)=1.

beats.prompt: The move that ran through this whole lesson.

feedback.correct: Cov(X,Y)=E[XY]−E[X]E[Y] — the product rule's residue. Its sign tells direction; zero only under independence. Next: how Cov adds inside Var(X+Y).
hints: Read each recap card.
The formula is a subtraction: observed joint average minus the independent baseline.
Tap to continue.
